title: Speaking & Doing
summary: Your first verbs and the prefixes that say who is acting.

teach.note: You can honestly say this now!

grammar.title: Pronoun prefixes: who is acting lives on the verb
grammar.body:
Cherokee verbs carry their subject as a **prefix** — there's no separate word for 'I' or 'you' in these sentences.
| ᏥᏬᏂᎭ tsi-woniha | **I** am speaking |
| ᎯᏬᏂᎭ hi-woniha | **you** are speaking |
| ᎦᏬᏂᎭ ga-woniha | **he/she** is speaking |
Swap the first syllable and you change who is doing the action. The stem -woniha ('speak') stays the same. This one pattern unlocks hundreds of verbs.

exercises.prompt: Which prefix means 'you' in these verbs?
Who is speaking in the word you heard?
Type the romanization for 'you are speaking'

teach.note: Same pattern as -woniha: g-/h-/∅ mark I / you / he-she.

grammar.title: Negation with tla
grammar.body:
To say you *don't* do something, put **Ꮭ** (tla) before the verb:
| ᎪᎵᎦ goliga | I understand |
| Ꮭ ᎪᎵᎦ tla goliga | I don't understand |
'Tla goliga' is one of the most useful phrases for a learner — use it freely, and pair it with ᏩᏙ (wado) when someone slows down for you.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I don't understand'?
